explanation: etc. Very silly or stylized animals calls for use in Cartoons.

discriminators: A vocalization with formant structure but exaggerated past biology: pitched higher and modulated far more wildly (large spectral_centroid_deviation_hz, extreme |pitch_slope_semitones_per_second|) than a real ANIMALS call. The unnatural excess IS the cartoon cue.
Non-human timbre with no syllabic speech rhythm separates it from CARTOON-VOCAL; text (duck, silly, toon) commits it.
Which animal it is can never be read from signal.

explanation: like for a bouncing cartoon character. Roger Rabbit or Tigger from Winnie the Pooh.

discriminators: A struck spring: a pitched tone whose f0 swings violently and REPEATEDLY after a single sharp attack - very high spectral_centroid_deviation_hz with a large pitch_slope_semitones_per_second that changes sign. Physically impossible in nature; the exaggeration is the signature.
CARTOON-TWANG bends pitch down once, monotonically, then dies; BOING keeps oscillating.
Tonal, so it is not CARTOON-IMPACT (broadband) and not CARTOON-SWISH (noise).

explanation: like a funny anvil hit. Most likely a very metallic resonant hit.

discriminators: Metal: bright, strongly inharmonic, and it RINGS - a long decay_time_seconds_60db is the material-impedance split from CARTOON-IMPACT, whose bonk dies immediately.
The cartoon exaggeration is an anvil ring that outlasts anything a real anvil would do.
Untuned and inharmonic in the large, unlike MUSICAL-BELLS, which is a tuned instrument played musically.

explanation: some overlap or confusion with CARTOON-STRETCH possible.

discriminators: Stick-slip contact: sustained but unstable - high spectral_flux and a wandering centroid, neither cleanly tonal nor cleanly noisy.

The UCS text flags overlap with CARTOON-STRETCH. The measurable split: STRETCH trends MONOTONICALLY in pitch/centroid (one-signed spectral_centroid_slope_hz_per_second); CREAK jitters around no trend.
Lower and longer than CARTOON-SQUEAK, which is a short, very high, thin tone.
A bulb/clown horn: a short, buzzy, HELD tone on a flat pitch plateau - a steady f0 is what separates it from CARTOON-WHISTLE and CARTOON-BOING, which glide or oscillate.
Harmonic and pitched, so not CARTOON-IMPACT.
Rasping, distorted harmonics and comic brevity push it away from MUSICAL-BRASS, which is an expressive musical performance.
A bonk: one broadband transient that dies fast. The SHORT decay_time_seconds_60db is the split from CARTOON-CLANG's long metallic ring.
Dry and low-banded, where CARTOON-SPLAT is wet - noisier, mid-banded, with a squelchy tail.
Bigger and longer than CARTOON-POP, which is a sub-200 ms blip.
A cartoon hit is an exaggerated impulse: crest_factor and envelope_skewness run higher than a real-world impact of the same size.

explanation: an overly comical sounding machine or contraption. A Rube Goldberg contraption.

discriminators: A Rube Goldberg contraption is a multi-event scene: a long chain of heterogeneous gags with partial mechanical periodicity, so transient_count is high and no single one-shot morphology fits.
If it is one gag noise, prefer the specific sibling (CARTOON-IMPACT, CARTOON-BOING, CARTOON-ZIP) - length_seconds and transient_count are the discriminator.
Object identity (machine vs vehicle) is text-only: CARTOON-VEHICLE is the same soup plus horns and engine sputters.

explanation: Cartoon sounds not fitting another category in this list.

discriminators: Abstain target for CARTOON: the file is clearly stylized and comic but matches no sibling morphology.
Never assert from signal.

explanation: Cartoon musical phrases. String plucks for a cartoon character sneaking up on another. Perhaps a cartoon musical phrase.

discriminators: A comic musical FIGURE (a sneak-up pizzicato line, a gliss): harmonic and multi-note, so transient_count > 1 and pitch moves in steps.
A gag, not a piece: shorter and unmetered next to MUSICAL-SONG & PHRASE and MUSICAL-LOOP (no beats_per_minute over bars).
One note only is CARTOON-PLUCK or CARTOON-TWANG, not this.
One plucked, pitched note with a fast harmonic decay - the same physics as MUSICAL-PLUCKED but isolated, dry and gag-length; only text and the comic exaggeration place it in CARTOON.
Pitch is STABLE: that is the split from CARTOON-TWANG (audible downward bend) and CARTOON-BOING (oscillating pitch).
Harmonic, so not CARTOON-IMPACT.

The smallest cartoon one-shot: a single tiny transient, typically well under 200 ms, with essentially no ring. length_seconds and decay_time_seconds_60db alone separate it from CARTOON-IMPACT and CARTOON-CLANG.
Dry and clean, where CARTOON-SPLAT is noisy, wet and several times longer.
A rattling grain train: dense, bright, unpitched micro-onsets with low crest_factor - noise, not a strike.
Measurably identical to MUSICAL-SHAKEN; only text (cartoony, toon, noisemaker) and the comic context place it here, so do not assert it from signal alone.
A sustained rattle rather than one hit rules out CARTOON-IMPACT.

explanation: funny liquid and mud like splats and things.

discriminators: A wet burst: noisy, mid-banded and unpitched, with a squelchy noisy tail instead of a clean decay - higher spectral_flatness and LOWER crest_factor than the dry, bass-heavy CARTOON-IMPACT.
Longer and messier than CARTOON-POP.
No harmonic content at all, unlike CARTOON-PLUCK or CARTOON-BOING.

explanation: Cartoon squeaks. funny little sounding squeaks of things.

discriminators: Rubber stick-slip: a very HIGH, thin, slightly unstable pitch with essentially zero low_band_energy - the missing bass is the strongest cue.
Higher, shorter and more pitched than CARTOON-CREAK (low, long, jittery, wooden).
Pitched, so not CARTOON-SHAKE (noise). If the pitch glides monotonically across a wide range it is CARTOON-WHISTLE or CARTOON-ZIP, not a squeak.

explanation: funny rubber or other type stretching sounds

discriminators: Rubber under tension: a sustained sound whose pitch and centroid climb MONOTONICALLY as it is pulled - a one-signed slope held over a second or more.
That trend is the split from CARTOON-CREAK, which jitters around no trend (the UCS text warns these two overlap).
CARTOON-ZIP has the same monotonic trajectory but is roughly an order of magnitude faster and far shorter.
Broadband noise moving past: UNPITCHED (near-zero harmonicity, high spectral_flatness) with a swept centroid and a smooth swell-and-fall envelope centred in the file.
CARTOON-ZIP is the pitched, much faster cousin - a swish stays noise.
Low crest_factor and no sharp transient rule out CARTOON-IMPACT.

explanation: that classic ruler twang type sound.

discriminators: A ruler or rubber-band twang: plucked attack whose pitch bends audibly DOWNWARD as tension relaxes. A negative pitch_slope_semitones_per_second is the discriminator against CARTOON-PLUCK, which holds a stable pitch.
It falls once and dies; CARTOON-BOING oscillates and reverses sign.
More inharmonic than a musical string (MUSICAL-PLUCKED) - a flapping ruler is not a tuned instrument.

explanation: think Jetsons spaceship or Flintstones cars.

discriminators: Object identity, not morphology: a comic vehicle is assembled from horns, sputters, rattles and whooshes, so no single morphology holds.
Signal can only rule out short one-shots; text (jetsons, flintstones, backfire) decides.
Indistinguishable from CARTOON-MACHINE by DSP - both are comic contraption scenes.

explanation: specifically very silly. See also CARTOON-ANIMALS for non-human vocals.

discriminators: Human vocal formants with syllabic modulation: a high voicing_ratio is what rules out every other CARTOON subcategory, none of which is voiced.
It differs from ordinary VOICES only by EXAGGERATION - an unnaturally wide pitch range and violent modulation - and by text (silly, toon, mumble, grumble).
Non-human, animal-like calls belong in CARTOON-ANIMAL.

A HELD tone under fast periodic frequency modulation (vibrato/wobble): sustained and harmonic, with rhythmic spectral_centroid_deviation_hz but NO net trend - pitch_slope_semitones_per_second averages near zero.
That zero net slope is the split from CARTOON-STRETCH and CARTOON-ZIP, which sweep monotonically.
CARTOON-BOING modulates far more violently and decays away; a warble just sits there wobbling.

explanation: other very silly whistles. A Wolf-Whistle.

discriminators: A near-sinusoidal, very high tone that GLIDES (slide whistle, wolf whistle): tiny partial_count, almost no low_band_energy, high pitch_hz, and a large |pitch_slope_semitones_per_second|.
Far purer (fewer partials) than CARTOON-HORN, which is buzzy and holds a flat pitch.
Pitched, so not CARTOON-SWISH (noise); slower and more musical than CARTOON-ZIP.

explanation: Cartoon zips. Very fast 'zip' type sounds

discriminators: The defining cartoon exaggeration: an EXTREME, very fast monotonic sweep - tens of semitones per second crammed into a few hundred milliseconds. No physical object moves like that; the impossible slope IS the label.
Same trajectory as CARTOON-STRETCH but roughly an order of magnitude faster and shorter.
More pitched and far steeper than CARTOON-SWISH, which stays broadband noise.
